display_name: Pacific Mail Steamship Company — Coastal and Transpacific Routes

segments.to: Portland, Oregon and Seattle/Tacoma, Washington Territory
segments.distance: approximately 650 miles to Portland; 800 miles to Seattle
segments.travel_time: 3-4 days to Portland; 4-5 days to Seattle (1870s-1890s)
segments.cost: $15-$25 cabin class to Portland; $20-$30 to Seattle (1880s). Steerage rates roughly half cabin fare.
segments.conditions: Coastal steamships hugged the Pacific shore, rounding Cape Mendocino and crossing the Columbia River bar — one of the most dangerous river entrances in the world, where Pacific swells met the outgoing Columbia current over shifting sandbars. Ships pitched and rolled in the fog that blanketed the coast for months. Passengers bound for Portland transferred to river steamers at Astoria for the final 100 miles up the Columbia and Willamette rivers. The run to Puget Sound continued past the Olympic Peninsula into the calm waters of the Sound, delivering settlers, supplies, and mail to lumber towns and fishing villages.

segments.distance: approximately 2,300 miles from San Francisco to Juneau; 1,500 miles from Seattle
segments.travel_time: 7-10 days from San Francisco; 5-7 days from Seattle (1890s-1900s)
segments.cost: $40-$75 cabin to Juneau (1897-1900, Klondike Gold Rush prices). Rates doubled and tripled during the Gold Rush peak of 1897-1898 as every seaworthy vessel was pressed into service.
segments.conditions: The Alaska run became a Gold Rush stampede route in 1897-1898 when news of the Klondike strike reached Seattle. Steamship companies loaded vessels beyond safe capacity with prospectors, sled dogs, pack horses, and a year's worth of provisions required by Canadian authorities for entry into the Yukon. Ships threaded through the Inside Passage — a thousand-mile corridor of fjords, islands, and glaciers between the Alaska mainland and the Alexander Archipelago — in waters where uncharted rocks, tidal currents, and sudden williwaw winds could drive a vessel ashore. Dozens of ships were lost on the Alaska run between 1897 and 1910.

segments.to: Yokohama, Japan and Hong Kong, China
segments.distance: approximately 4,500 miles to Yokohama; 7,000 miles to Hong Kong
segments.travel_time: 18-22 days to Yokohama; 28-35 days to Hong Kong (1870s-1900s)
segments.cost: $50-$100 cabin to Yokohama; steerage $30-$50 (1880s-1890s). Chinese laborers traveling under the credit-ticket system paid equivalent rates guaranteed by the Six Companies.
segments.conditions: The transpacific steamers of the Pacific Mail Steamship Company — vessels like the City of Peking and the China — were iron-hulled sidewheelers and later steel screw steamships that carried a rigid class hierarchy. First-class passengers dined at the captain's table. Steerage passengers, predominantly Chinese, occupied the lower decks in conditions little better than the sailing ships they replaced. The Great Circle route from San Francisco to Yokohama passed through the North Pacific storm belt, where winter gales could delay a crossing by days and send waves crashing over the bow of a 4,000-ton steamship.

who_used_it: Chinese immigrants traveling between San Francisco and Hong Kong (both directions). Japanese picture brides and laborers bound for Hawaiian sugar plantations and Pacific Coast farms. Klondike Gold Rush prospectors heading to Alaska (1897-1900). Pacific Northwest settlers traveling from San Francisco to Portland, Seattle, and Tacoma. Salmon cannery workers — Chinese, Japanese, Filipino, and Native Alaskan — shuttled seasonally between San Francisco and Alaska. U.S. military personnel and government officials traveling to Asia.
narrative_hook: In {year}, {ancestor_name} boarded a Pacific Mail steamship at {origin}, joining the flow of emigrants, prospectors, and laborers that the company funneled up and down the Pacific coast and across the ocean to {destination} — a route where the fog never lifted for long and the company ledgers counted passengers the same way they counted cargo.
source_references: John Haskell Kemble, 'The Panama Route, 1848-1869' (University of California Press, 1943)
Robert J. Chandler, 'Pacific Mail Steamship Company' (California History, Vol. 57, No. 1, 1978)
Ronald Takaki, 'Strangers from a Different Shore: A History of Asian Americans' (Little, Brown and Company, 1989)
Pierre Berton, 'The Klondike Fever: The Life and Death of the Last Great Gold Rush' (Alfred A. Knopf, 1958)
